abstract: Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS) are persistent environmental pollutants linked to severe health risks, yet detecting them at regulated ultra-trace levels (ng/L) typically requires costly, lab-based chromatography and mass spectrometry. This study introduces a portable sensing platform using amplifying fluorescent polymers (AFPs) designed for high-sensitivity aqueous PFAS detection. We synthesized a series of highly fluorinated poly(p-phenylene ethynylene) and polyfluorene derivatives incorporating pyridine-based selectors. These selectors undergo a proton-transfer reaction upon binding with acidic PFAS like PFOA and PFOS. The fluorinated segments of the polymer facilitate analyte partitioning into the material, while the resulting pyridinium groups act as low-energy exciton traps, causing a significant red-shift in fluorescence that is amplified by the conjugated backbone. Evaluated as thin films and aqueous conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CPdots), the sensors achieved detection limits of approximately 1 ppb and 100 ppt, respectively. Notably, the AFPs demonstrated high selectivity for acidic PFAS over non-fluorinated acids and remained effective across various matrices, including milliQ, deionized, and well water. This approach offers a robust, scalable strategy for the on-site monitoring of PFAS in distributed water infrastructures.
